title: Ammunition consumption
principles: Ammunition pressure should be evaluated from consumption rate, reserve capacity, pickup, and weapon-specific cost rather than from fire rate alone.
High fire rate may increase consumption, but the practical result depends on whether each attack or tick consumes ammunition and how much is recovered.
A large reserve delays depletion but does not prove long-duration efficiency.
Ammo pickup can offset high consumption when supplied values support that relationship.
Beam and continuous weapons may use ammunition per tick or through another weapon-specific rule; do not assume one unit per displayed fire-rate event.
Do not recommend ammunition efficiency as a dominant correction when pickup or cost information is absent unless the mechanic explicitly identifies a resource problem.
Use the term ammunition or munición, not projectiles, when discussing reserves or consumption.

low: The supplied consumption signal does not indicate notable reserve pressure.
moderate: Consumption may require attention during repeated use but is not clearly dominant.
elevated: The firing pattern suggests increased consumption, but final efficiency still depends on pickup and cost data.
undetermined: The supplied fields are insufficient to classify ammunition efficiency.

exceptions: Do not infer ammunition starvation from fire rate alone.
Do not assume multishot consumes additional ammunition.
Do not infer per-tick beam cost when it is absent.
